item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple yet effective: You really can't trust a car salesman +1

Looking back on this article now, I would like us all to consider for a moment the plight of the people at the IRS and the SEC who have to deal with parafinance like this. Just from Third Law, there's also:

  • SCP-2630 — Prometheus built a mechanical haruspex to predict changes in the stock market, and possibly also cause them.
  • The Necromancer — Our friends at PLabs summon the ghost of John Keynes to engage in insider trading.

Between time travel, necromancy, augury, and who knows what else PLabs cooked up, trying to properly audit them looks like a Sisyphean task. Would really love to see a tale about it though.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Interesting and I like the concept, but I feel like a third law article type expansion going about using this as a learning example and using larger loops to make the business more recession proof and other methodologies would be more interesting than a "mainline Skip".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an example of the beauty of utilizing the horror of time paradoxes to create something simple yet unsettling. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much this, yeah. The addition of CD players to cars wasn't a huge innovation, but having that a year before everybody else would still give you a pretty nice edge. There are lots and lots of little bits of tech added to cars every year that this can be applied to, and if your main market is luxury vehicles, you're probably gonna be focusing on those little features anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Remember that Argo is using the sales of the cars to finance their production in the first place, and more advanced technology is also more expensive; if the technology is too advanced, the sales would be unable to sustain the production and it would draw unwanted attention to their operation. A one-year-difference still makes the cars significantly better than the ones produced by Argo's competitors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My main problem with it is that one year does not make that much difference in technology. Neutral vote.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Following the addition of the temporal tag again, I have locked the page at the author's request. If you spot any mechanical errors, please make a comment here and it will be fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh, this was a fun skip to wrap my head around. +1
I never know what to say for these milestone posts. Hooray, upvotes?

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Reverted the addition of the temporal tag by Salamander724, as this skip does not "affects, or is otherwise related to the passage of time." The anomaly itself is not temporal in any way.

In the future, please do not make changes to the tagging of this article without consulting me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whether this is due to precautions taken by Argo Automotives or due to neutralization of paradoxes by an alternate timeline Foundation is unknown.

Thad Xyank tale involving him finding out all about this. I demand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good and you should feel good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is almost exactly the central premise of the Third Law canon that I'm working on (which this skip is set-in/part-of).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could be that one year was the cost-effective time-limit. Say, it would cost too much in power or faerie extract or whatever to send all those cars back two years and still make a profit. On the other point, this one doesn't give a date for PL's liquidation, just Argo's. Large corporate conglomerates tend to collapse slowly like that- PL's main research branch folding around 1998 with some sort of major disaster at their flagship laboratory is one of those sort-of-canon things, but that wasn't the end of the company. This branch limped along until 2009. There might even still be a few remaining PL branches out there still, producing vaguely anomalous transistors for Anderson Robotics or replacement parts for Kant Counters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like this, but why didn't the car makers go further into the past? Was it safer this way? Also, I don't like it giving an exact year when Prometheus was liquified.

Anyway, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is literally futures trading.

In the future.

Ugh. Take your +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very deftly written. Charmingly simple yet intriguing. One of the very few scips Ive felt compelled to comment on recently. Well done, sir/madam.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why, oh why did I go away on vacation in early April? I missed this jewel!!!!

For my whole life I've been thinking about this buying-car-models-of-next-year stuff in terms of time travel, just for shits 'n giggles. Except when I bought my own new car… that was in February! So it's doubly fun to see an SCP dealing with this.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: No. No, not really.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
*At a loss for words other than these.

Better?

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I remember when my highest-rated hit 100 and then wobbled there for a bit.

Fun times. Fun times. Congrats, anyways.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
Are you really at a loss for words?

"I am literally at a loss for words" are words.

I am literally at a loss for words.

Edit: And then literally the second I post this it goes back down to 99.

Edit: And now it's back at 100. My poor heart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, a lot.

I had some initial suspension of disbelief issues (imagining a completely new car company is iffy for me unless we're talking super-luxury brands, and I initially thought it was just a joke about how dealerships stock the '09 models in '08), but you've managed to seal up the narrative and just about tie a bow on it while still leaving enough unresolved questions to keep me scratching my head. Bemusement and just the beginning of fridge-logic kicking in is a good place to end an article on.

The format screw with the pre-neutralized tag really helped you keep the overall package nice and compact, and I think the cleanness and conciseness were really beneficial, especially for a post-PL property.

+1 enthusiastically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noting that I reverted the addition of the paradox tag, as this skip neither "generates, exhibits, or is involved in paradoxical events or situations". That's rather the entire point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Likewise, I'd like to see the board meeting where they come to the conclusion that they have to make this year's cars to send into the past, because from their standpoint they already did it. And then have that one guy who thinks, well, what if we don't? What's the worst a paradox could do?

And the one guy who meekly suggests multiverse theory and gets shouted down immediately.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Wait, how does that work? Unless you get the profits in cash form, and send them back/go forward to collect them, how would you get anyone to recognize the existence of your funds?

I guess it could be a service offered by a time travel capable bank or broker/dealer, but the fees would probably be murder. Argo Automotive probably took the safer para-economic route.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I originally used it late next year. Is it plagiarism if you both stole it from me but then used a Temporal Posting Loop to be chronologically first?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's funny about this, for me, is that they're not even super advanced cars. Just like Chevrolet or Ford will manufacture cars and sell them a model year newer, this company did the exact same thing and went a step further, manufacturing them later and selling them earlier.

Honestly, it's almost more honest in my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this a day after I created something similar to this using Venezuela's dysfunctional economical system.

1: get 100$ and trade it at the BM market to acquire about 104.000Bs (1$=1040Bs) in cash.
2: Travel back to 2009 (we had a paper money swap that year) and trade your Bs (6.4Bs=1$) for 16.250$. If you reinvest it at the same rate, you'll get about 2.640,625$ at the end of the cycle.
3: Repeat over and over until you can buy the planet (or send Venezuela into bankrupt all by yourself).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so short that if you went back in time a year, you could've posted this for the short story contest in '15.

Now that reminds me of this story. Time travel is awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so simple yet so smart.

item.title: Re: +50
item.content:encoded:
You've really done it now Skippy.
You broke the internet.

… in under 12 hours.

I don't even know anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted at the second footnote.
Paraeconomics is an interesting field to look into.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tells a story in a fantastic way. :D I entirely love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would love to see that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically, they wisely weren't willing to gamble that just ignoring the paradox would be okay: They had the cars, therefore the cars had to be made and sent back in time.

In the immortal words of Cave Johnson:

If you meet yourself on the testing track, don't make eye contact. Lab boys tell me that'll wipe out time. Entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brings a whole new meaning to stock futures. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a short, simple, and fun skip. This also one that is totally in character for a PL subsidiary.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole process of the identification of the anomaly itself could be nice tale fodder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've got an idea for a PL spinoff that was started by the financial wonks, playing off the kind of real life mad economics that led to the housing crash.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
True, but if there's one thing I've learned from my experiences in bioengineering and finance, it's that you can't get your MBA while you get your Ph.D.

(I've seen plenty of egghead startups go under because despite their stars' pioneering scientific progress, they had no business sense. So, given that we're talking Prometheus Labs, I'm not too horribly surprised.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The smart thing would have been to take a bunch of loans to cover the suspiciously missing debt, paid the minimum interest and shuffled the rest into profits, made an IPO in 2007, and sold the company to escape with a ton of money.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, that seems overengineered.

In other words, perfectly in-character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not quite. The cars they sold in 2007 were the 2008 year model, and were made in 2008. In 2008, they receive the 2009 year model cars from the future, but are unable to sell enough of them to make a profit due to the global recession. However, they still have to make the cars in 2009 to send back to 2008, because otherwise, where did the cars they got in 2008 come from? They end up making all of the 2009 year model cars and send them back in time, but go bankrupt in the process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This makes my head hurt. +1.

Fridge logic time (inevitable with any sort of time-travel). So, looking at this chronologically according to the base timeline:

In 2007, they were producing the 2007 vehicles and sending them back in time to 2006, while receiving the 2008 vehicles and selling them.

But then in 2008, they wouldn't have received any 2009 vehicles (since they didn't / wouldn't sell in 2008). Without the new inventory to sell, there's no revenue with which to manufacture the new inventory they didn't receive.

They use the revenue from the sale of the 2008 vehicles to produce the 2008 vehicles anyway, in order to prevent a time-paradox. But they know that once they finish, they will be bankrupt.

So, I'm not understanding why or how they produced the 2009 vehicles. They just never would have received them from the future, and thus would have known not to produce them (and not had the money to do so even if they wanted to, since they hadn't had any to sell).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It's a simple, neat idea. I really enjoy what little of your PL stuff I have read. I should probably go seeking it out

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Also, a special mention is deserved by sirpudding, who originally pitched the idea of using time travel as an investment strategy a few months ago. While this skip emerged from an unrelated discussion in chat yesterday, I can't say that his pitch didn't have some influence on this.

Well this is a bit different because I was envisioning a purely financial instrument, where you borrow from your future profits without interest in order to cover costs in the present, whereas this involves shipping cars to the past in order to cover the costs of manufacturing them in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah! What if the Foundation caused the financial crisis to stop Argo's plans?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And they would've gotten away with it too if it weren't for you meddling bankers!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see you used my Self-Funding Temporal Finance Loop. I nearly did myself as a PL format, I guess it's good that I didn't as it would be redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ha!

I like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I think the Foundation will attempt a corporate takeover of Argo and change it into a normal car company after purging all former Prometheus Lab members.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As it is, they're rather lucky that the whole thing resolved itself as well as it did.

Of course, that's because we can only possibly exist in a universe that didn't temporally unravel five years ago.

You'd be amazed at the amount of detritus piled up outside our universe because of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Considering the cars themselves are only built with technology a year ahead of their peers, they don't pose a huge threat to normalcy. And, considering the delicate nature of time, the Foundation would probably be pretty cautious in their handling of the whole thing. I expect that they'd have allowed Argo to continue operating while they slowly reduced their use of the time loop. It's entirely possible that the Foundation might have even started funding Argo to reduce its reliance on temporally-obtained funds.

As it is, they're rather lucky that the whole thing resolved itself as well as it did.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: I like what you've done with it. I give it a solid upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I work in corporate finance and am well-acquainted with the risks associated with carrying forward revenue recognition. This is beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have my doubts when I saw the general lack of procedures (Even Neutralised SCPs have them, usually). But the rest of the article works out fine.

On that note, what would have been this SCP's procedures if it were discovered while active?
